title: We automated our own operations first
excerpt: Before we sold AI employees to anyone else, we built them for ourselves. Some worked immediately. Some flopped. Here's what we learned.
author: Simon Insinger
content:
## Eating our own cooking

When Alex and I started CreateStaff, we had a rule: don't sell anything we haven't used ourselves.

So before we took on a single client, we built AI employees for our own two-person operation. We were the guinea pigs. Some of what we built worked right away. Some of it was genuinely bad. Here's what actually happened.

## What worked immediately

Email triage. This was the first thing we automated and it worked from day one. Both of us were drowning in inbox noise — newsletters, cold outreach, notification emails mixed in with actual client messages. The AI sorted everything into categories and surfaced what mattered. Within a week, neither of us had to look at our full inbox anymore.

Meeting prep. Before every call, the AI pulls together context — who the person is, what we last talked about, any relevant notes. This sounds small but it saved us from showing up to calls and fumbling through "remind me what we discussed last time." Embarrassing how much that was happening before.

Research for proposals. When a potential client reaches out, we need to understand their business fast. The AI does a sweep — company info, team size, recent news, tech stack when it's public — and puts together a one-pager. Used to take an hour. Now takes about 30 seconds.

## What flopped

Automated outbound. We tried having AI write and send cold outreach on our behalf. The quality was fine — the messages read well, personalization was solid. But something felt off about it. We're a company that builds AI employees, and having AI do our own sales outreach felt like it undermined the personal touch we were trying to sell. We pulled it after two weeks.

Maybe we'll bring it back eventually, but for now our outbound is human-written.

Project management. We tried building an AI that would track our tasks, send reminders, and keep projects on schedule. It mostly just annoyed us. With a two-person team, we know what we're working on. The overhead of feeding context to an AI project manager wasn't worth the coordination it provided. This one probably works better for bigger teams.

Client communication drafting. The AI could draft client update emails, but we always rewrote them anyway. Our clients are trusting us with something important and the communication needs to sound like us, not like a template. We ended up spending more time editing drafts than we would have spent writing from scratch.

## The lessons

A few things we learned that now shape how we build for clients.

Start with information processing, not communication. AI is great at reading, sorting, and summarizing. It's less great at writing things that need to sound like a specific person. Triage before drafting. Research before outreach.

Two-person teams and 200-person teams have different needs. What's overhead for us is a lifesaver for a company with 50 people and no chief of staff. We stopped assuming our experience mapped directly to our clients'.

The best AI employees do boring work. The exciting-sounding use cases ("AI strategist!") flopped. The boring ones ("sort my email") worked great. There's a lesson in there about where AI actually adds value versus where it just sounds impressive.

Failure is information. Every thing we tried that didn't work taught us something we now use when scoping client projects. If we hadn't tried the outbound experiment, we wouldn't know where the line is between useful automation and things that feel weird.

## Why this matters for you

We're not pitching something we built in theory. Every AI employee we sell is a variation on something we've tested on ourselves. When we say "this works," it's because we've run it. When we say "this role isn't a great fit for AI yet," that's usually because we tried it and it wasn't.
